coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence recovery based on synergetic effect for ALP detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Siqi
Yang, Weishan
Ye, Silu
Cao, Shijie
Li, Yifang
Wei, Zhaoying
Yan Ngai, Ka
Dai, Junbiao
Mao, Guobin
Ma, Yingxin
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important biomarker associated with diabetes, liver dysfunction, bone diseases, and breast cancer. Here we developed a method based on synergetic fluorescence recovery for the sensitive detection of ALP. Cadmium-zinc-selenium (CdZnSe) quantum dots (QDs) were prepared by one-pot water bath method without any complicated and rigorous conditions. CdZnSe QDs displayed high luminous efficiency, good stability, and good biocompatibility. KMnO4 and ascorbic acid phosphate (AAP) can dynamically quench the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs. Ascorbic acid, produced by ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP, reacted with KMnO4, causing the synergetic fluorescence recovery of CdZnSe QDs. The synergetic recovery efficiency correlates well with the logarithmic ALP concentration in the range of 2.5–250 U/L with a detection limit of 0.21 U/L. In addition, good recoveries were obtained in the detection of ALP in human serum. This method provided a new research idea to improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity of ALP detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Alkaline phosphatase
Sensor
Turn-off–on
Synergetic fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on the stability of CdZnSe QDs
Synergetic quenching effect of KMnO4 and AAP
Detection of ALP by CdZnSe QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption (solid line) and fluorescence (dashed line) spectrum of CdZnSe QDs.

(A) TEM image of CdZnSe QDs. Insets: HRTEM image. (B) The size distribution of CdZnSe QDs measured by TEM. (C) XRD pattern of CdZnSe QDs in comparison with the standard pattern of CdSe (JCPDS No.19–0191) and ZnSe (JCPDS No.37–1463) cubic phases. (D) FT-IR spectra of CdZnSe QDs and glutathione. (E) XPS of CdZnSe QDs. (F) Hydrodynamic size distribution of CdZnSe QDs.

(A) Photo-stability of CdZnSe QDs and CdTe QDs. (B) Anti-interference ability of CdZnSe QDs in buffer containing different cations and amino acids (pH 9.5). (C) Fluorescence intensity of CdZnSe QDs at different pH. (D) Fluorescence intensity of CdZnSe QDs in serum with different dilutions.
Fluorescence responses of CdZnSe QDs in the present of KMnO4, AAP, or KMnO4 + AAP.

(A) Fluorescence responses of CdZnSe QDs after adding AAP (125 μM), KMnO4 (10 μM), and ALP with different concentrations. (B) Fluorescence intensity at 443 nm vs ALP concentration and its calibration curve.
Illustration for (A) the synthesis of CdZnSe QDs, and (B) the synergetic quenching effect of KMnO4 and AAP (a, b and c) on CdZnSe QDs, and the principle of ALP detection (d).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence recovery based on synergetic effect for ALP detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Synthetic Genomics and Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Synthetic Genomics. Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Synthetic Genomics and Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Synthetic Genomics

Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology, Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen, 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important biomarker associated with diabetes, liver dysfunction, bone diseases, and breast cancer. Here we developed a method based on synergetic fluorescence recovery for the sensitive detection of ALP. Cadmium-zinc-selenium (CdZnSe) quantum dots (QDs) were prepared by one-pot water bath method without any complicated and rigorous conditions. CdZnSe QDs displayed high luminous efficiency, good stability, and good biocompatibility. KMnO4 and ascorbic acid phosphate (AAP) can dynamically quench the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs. Ascorbic acid, produced by ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP, reacted with KMnO4, causing the synergetic fluorescence recovery of CdZnSe QDs. The synergetic recovery efficiency correlates well with the logarithmic ALP concentration in the range of 2.5–250 U/L with a detection limit of 0.21 U/L. In addition, good recoveries were obtained in the detection of ALP in human serum. This method provided a new research idea to improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity of ALP detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important hydrolase, generally distributed in mammalian body fluids and tissues[1]. It can remove phosphate groups in various biomolecules such as nucleic acids and proteins through dephosphorylation[2]. Normally, the ALP concentration is 40–190 U/L in adult blood, and it can reach 500 U/L in children and pregnant women[3]. The abnormal activity of ALP in human serum is related to numerous diseases, such as diabetes, liver dysfunction, bone diseases, and breast cancer[4,5]. In addition, ALP is widely used as labeling enzyme for enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay due to its broad substrate specificity, good stability, high catalytic efficiency, and low cost[6–8]. Therefore, it is crucial to propose a simple, and sensitive method to determine ALP activity for clinical diagnosis. Among several strategies for detecting ALP activity, such as colorimetry[9], chemiluminescence[10,11], or electrochemiluminescence[12–14], electrochemistry[15,16], and fluorescence[17–20], fluorescence analysis is considered as the most commonly analytical method due to its advantages of high sensitivity, convenient operation, and low instrument requirements[21].

Fluorescence analysis is performed in two main modes, turn-off and turn-on, depending on fluorescence quenching and recovery, respectively[22,23]. Mao et al. fabricated a fluorescence turn-off system for ALP detection based on fluorescence quenching of CdTe/CdS quantum dots (QDs) [18]. The accuracy and sensitivity of the fluorescence turn-off system need to be improved. Recently, Zhao et al. proposed a new method for detecting ALP, where the reaction was catalyzed by ALP hydrolysis of hydroxyphenyl phosphate sodium to produce azamonardine[24]. Fluorescence turn-on method can effectively reduce environmental interference and improve detection sensitivity. The combination of the above two modes, the fluorescence turn-off–on system, displays advantages of high precision, low interference, and high selectivity. In most turn-off–on systems, the probe fluorescence is first decreased by unique quencher and then recovered by reaction between the quencher and the analyte. However, signal transduction based on a single quencher is limited by quenching sensitivity. To our knowledge, two types of quenchers have not been used for fluorescence turn-off, and they are consumed together after analyte activated fluorescence turn-on.

In this study, we designed a simple and sensitive method for the detection of ALP through synergetic fluorescence recovery of cadmium-zinc-selenium (CdZnSe) QDs. The probe was prepared by a simple and rapid water bath method, which operated at 95 °C for 30 min, without any complicated and rigorous conditions, such as nitrogen atmosphere protection, or high temperature and pressure (Scheme 1 A) [25–27]. In addition, CdZnSe QDs displayed various advantages, including high luminous efficiency, good stability, and good biocompatibility. Scheme 1B shows that both ascorbic acid phosphate (AAP) and KMnO4 can quench the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs through dynamic quenching. Ascorbic acid (AA), the hydrolysate of AAP catalyzed by ALP, can react with KMnO4, resulting in the synergetic fluorescence recovery of CdZnSe QDs by simultaneous consumption of AAP and KMnO4. The fluorescence recovery was used for the detection of ALP activity. The establishment of this method provided a new research idea for improving the sensitivity of ALP detection.

CdCl2, Zn(OAc)2, NaBH4, glutathione (GSH), NaOH, ascorbic acid phosphate (AAP), Se powder, and KMnO4 were purchased from Sigma. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was purchased from New England Biolabs. Human serum was obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University. The fluorescence spectra were measured using the F-4700 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi). The UV–vis spectra were measured using Agilent. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was observed with JEM 2100 microscope (JEOL Ltd.). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on the Ultima IV XRD system (Rigaku). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected with K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was measured using IRAffinity-1 s spectrometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained using Edinburgh FLS1000 (Edinburgh Instruments).

CdZnSe QDs were prepared by one-pot water bath method. Briefly, 85 mg of CdCl2 and 150 mg of GSH were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water to prepare Cd precursor. Then, 85 mg of Zn(Ac)2 and 150 mg of GSH was dissolved with 40 mL of deionized water to prepare Zn precursor. The pH of the two precursors were adjusted to 11.0 with NaOH. 20 mg of Se powder and 20 mg of NaBH4 were added and dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water to produce NaHSe solution. Zn precursor and Cd precursor were mixed in a ratio of 9:1, and quickly mixed with fresh NaHSe solution in a ratio of 4.5:1, placing in a 95 °C water bath for 30 min. After cooling to room temperature, the raw CdZnSe QDs were obtained by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min). CdZnSe QDs were concentrated with isopropanol and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for XRD and XPS analysis.

The cytotoxicity of CdZnSe QDs was studied by MTS assay (Promega). 293 T cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (4000 cells/well), each well containing 100 μL DMEM and cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. QDs were added to wells with final concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 60, 120, 200, and 600 nM, and incubated for 24 h. Then, the supernatant was removed and added with 90 μL of fresh DMEM. MTS reaction solution (10 μL) was added and incubated for 4 h. The absorbance intensity was collected (Bio-Rad, USA).

To study the quenching effect, 2 μL of AAP (50 mM) and 20 μL of KMnO4 (400 μM) were added to Tris buffer (pH 9.5, 20 mM) containing 20 nM of CdZnSe QDs. The total volume of the reaction solution was 800 μL. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was collected after incubation for 60 min. 350 nm was used as excitation wavelength.

Different concentrations of ALP were added to Tris buffer (pH 9.5, 20 mM) containing 125 μM of AAP, and the total volume was 380 μL. The mixed solution was reacted at 37 °C for 40 min. Then, 10 μL of KMnO4 (400 μM) was added and incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. 10 μL of CdZnSe QDs (800 nM) was added and reacted for 20 min at 25 °C. The fluorescence spectrum of the solution was collected with excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

A novel one-pot water bath method was developed for the preparation of CdZnSe QDs without any requirement of oxygen-free conditions. Due to the different band energy of ZnSe and CdSe, the ratio of Zn2+ and Cd2+ will seriously influence the emission wavelength of CdZnSe QDs. The fluorescence spectra of CdZnSe QDs with different ratios of Zn2+ and Cd2+ were investigated (Figure S1A). With the increase of ratio of Zn2+ and Cd2+, the emission wavelength of CdZnSe shifted to red. However, the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs was poor when excessive Cd precursor was added. CdZnSe QDs with the highest fluorescence efficiency were obtained, when Zn2+/Cd2+ were 19:1. As shown in Figure S1B, when the ratio of M2+ and Se2- was 4.5:1, the fluorescence intensity was the highest. The optimized ratio of M2+ and GSH showed that when the ratio of M2+ and GSH was 1:1, the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained (Figure S2). The optimization of the reaction pH revealed that the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained when the pH value was 11 (Figure S3). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of CdZnSe QDs showed an absorption peak and fluorescence peak at 412 nm and 446 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 ). The fluorescence half peak width was 31 nm, indicating that the CdZnSe QDs had good dispersion. The concentration of CdZnSe QDs was measured according to the previous report [28].

The morphology, size, and crystal lattice structure of CdZnSe QDs were observed by TEM and HR-TEM. CdZnSe QDs showed a spherical structure with good dispersion, and the average size is about 3.1 nm. HR-TEM revealed the crystal lattice of CdZnSe QDs (Fig. 2 A-2B). XRD analysis of the crystal lattice showed that the degree of CdZnSe QDs was greater than CdSe and less than ZnSe, indicating that the CdZnSe QDs may be alloyed (Fig. 2C). FT-IR spectra of GSH and GSH-stabilized CdZnSe QDs were recorded to study the interaction between GSH and metal ions. The peak of -SH at 2534 cm−1 disappeared after CdZnSe QDs growth, indicating that the interaction between -SH and metal ions played an important role in QD synthesis (Fig. 2D). Then, XPS analysis of the surface elementary composition of CdZnSe QDs showed typical distinguishable characteristic peaks of Se (3d), S (2p), Cd (3d), and Zn (2p) demonstrating the presence of Zn, Cd, Se, and S in CdZnSe QDs (Fig. 2E). The hydrodynamic size of CdZnSe QDs was 6.7 nm, which was larger than that of CdZnSe QDs (3.1 nm) measured by TEM (Fig. 2F). The cytotoxicity of CdZnSe QDs on 293 T cells was studied by MTS assay, and the results showed no adverse effect of CdZnSe QDs on 293 T cells (Figure S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the stability of CdZnSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of CdTe QDs and CdZnSe QDs was evaluated by irradiation with Xe lamp (λex = 350 nm) in air for 60 min. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs remained just 30% and the fluorescence intensity of CdZnSe QDs remained over 80%, indicating the good photostability of CdZnSe QDs (Fig. 3 A). Chemical stability was studied by adding different types of ions or amino acids. Zn2+ slightly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CdZnSe QDs, while other ions and amino acids had no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3B). These results showed that CdZnSe QDs had satisfactory chemical stability in Tris buffer containing common ions and amino acids. The fluorescence stability of CdZnSe QDs was further evaluated at different pH values. When the pH value was 9.5, the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained, and when the pH value was 7.5–11.5, the fluorescence intensity maintained good (Fig. 3C). The effect of fetal bovine serum (FBS) on the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, when 1% FBS was added to the CdZnSe QDs solution, the fluorescence did not change, which indicated the good stability in complex serum environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic quenching effect of KMnO4 and AAP
ce:section.ce:para: In traditional method, fluorescence analysis is performed by the increase or decrease in fluorescence intensity by using a single sensitizer and quencher, respectively. In this study, we found that both AAP and KMnO4 can quench the fluorescence of CdZnSe QDs (Figure S5). The synergetic quenching effect made fluorescence quenching more effective than using a single quencher (Fig. 4 ). The quenching mechanism of CdZnSe QDs by KMnO4 and AAP was studied by time-solved fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence lifetime of CdZnSe QDs decreased significantly after the addition of KMnO4 or AAP (Figure S6). The absorption peak of CdZnSe QDs hardly changed with the addition of AAP or KMnO4 (Figure S7). The time-solved fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra demonstrated the dynamic quenching of CdZnSe QDs by KMnO4 and AAP. A simple and sensitive method for the detection of ALP was constructed through the reaction of KMnO4 with AA produced by ALP-mediated hydrolysis of AAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP by CdZnSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of AAP hydrolysis catalyzed by ALP was optimized. The quenching efficiency was maximum at pH of 9.5 (Figure S8). When KMnO4 and AAP were added, the strong fluorescence of CdZnSe QD was effectively quenched to 50% by the synergetic quenching effect. ALP-mediated hydrolysis and KMnO4 oxidation resulted in the simultaneous consumption of KMnO4 and AAP after adding ALP, and the fluorescence recovery was rapid and sensitive. Fluorescence spectra of CdZnSe QDs containing AAP (125 μM), KMnO4 (10 μM), and ALP with different concentrations were obtained. The fluorescence intensity of CdZnSe QDs at 440 nm increased with the increase of ALP concentration (Fig. 5 A). The synergetic recovery efficiency correlates well with the logarithmic ALP concentration in the range of 2.5–250 U/L with a detection limit of 0.21 U/L (Fig. 5B). Therefore, the method provided better sensitivity than most ALP detection methods previously reported (Table S1).

To evaluate the selectivity of CdZnSe QDs in the detection of ALP, various potential enzymes in human serum or medical detection systems were studied, including T4 ligase, Taq enzyme, horseradish peroxidase, catalase, and proteinase K. Other enzymes had no obvious effect on fluorescence, even though their concentration was 10 times higher than that of ALP (Figure S9). The result showed that our method had excellent selectivity for ALP detection.

To explore the practical application of CdZnSe-AAP-KMnO4 system in complex biological samples, ALP spiked in human serum was determined. Human serum was provided by the First Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University and diluted to 2% to weaken the interference from serum, and different concentrations of ALP (10, 50, and 100 U/L) were added. As listed in Table S2, the recoveries and relative standard deviations were in the range of 91.60–102.32% and 3.45–9.12%, respectively, indicating that our proposed system can rapidly and precisely detect ALP in biological samples such as human serum.

We proposed a simple and sensitive method for the determination of ALP activity through the quenching effect of KMnO4 and AAP on CdZnSe QDs and the ALP-responsive synergetic fluorescence recovery. CdZnSe QDs were prepared by a one-pot water bath method, which did not involve complicated and rigorous conditions, including nitrogen atmosphere protection or high temperature and high pressure. Furthermore, CdZnSe QDs exhibited high luminous efficiency, good stability, and low cytotoxicity. The quenching effect of KMnO4 and AAP on CdZnSe QDs and synergetic fluorescence recovery were applied for ALP detection. The synergetic recovery efficiency correlates well with the logarithmic ALP concentration in the range of 2.5–250 U/L with a detection limit of 0.21 U/L, which provided good sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, good recoveries and relative standard deviations were obtained in the detection of ALP in human serum. Therefore, this method provided a novel research insight for improving the detection sensitivity.

Siqi Huang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Weishan Yang: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Silu Ye: Data curation. Shijie Cao: Data curation. Yifang Li: Formal analysis. Zhaoying Wei: Investigation. Ka Yan Ngai: Investigation. Junbiao Dai: Funding acquisition, Resources. Guobin Mao: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yingxin Ma: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFA0910900), National Natural Science Foundation (22104147), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2021359), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong (2018B030306046, 2020A1515111130), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Synthetic Genomics (2019B030301006), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (KQTD20180413181837372), and Shenzhen Outstanding Talents Training Fund.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121550.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase based on the use of ssDNA-templated fluorescent silver nanoclusters and on enzyme-triggered silver reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. He, B.N. Jiao, Determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase based on the use of ssDNA-templated fluorescent silver nanoclusters and on enzyme-triggered silver reduction, Microchim Acta, 184 (2017) 4167-4173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzymatic hydrogelation-induced fluorescence turn-off for sensing alkaline phosphatase in vitro and in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dong, Q.Q. Miao, Z.J. Hai, Y. Yuan, G.L. Liang, Enzymatic hydrogelation-induced fluorescence turn-off for sensing alkaline phosphatase in vitro and in living cells, Anal Chem, 87 (2015) 6475-6478.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.L. Li, H.L. Fu, X.J. Chen, P.W. Gong, G. Chen, L. Xia, H. Wang, J.M. You, Y.N. Wu, Facile and sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect, Anal Chem, 88 (2016) 2720-2726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA-templated silver nanoclusters for fluorometric determination of the activity and inhibition of alkaline phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.Y. Guo, D.L. Chen, M.H. Yang, DNA-templated silver nanoclusters for fluorometric determination of the activity and inhibition of alkaline phosphatase, Microchim Acta, 184 (2017) 2165-2170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect based fluorometric determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase by using carbon dots codoped with boron and nitrogen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mao, T. Tian, Y. He, Y.L. Ge, J.G. Zhou, G.W. Song, Inner filter effect based fluorometric determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase by using carbon dots codoped with boron and nitrogen, Microchim Acta, 185 (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning surface states to achieve the modulated fluorescence of carbon dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and immunoassay of alpha-fetoprotein

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Fang, X.Q. Li, H. Wang, X.M. Wu, G.L. Wang, Tuning surface states to achieve the modulated fluorescence of carbon dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and immunoassay of alpha-fetoprotein, Sensor Actuat B-Chem, 257 (2018) 620-628.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence immunoassay based on the phosphate-triggered fluorescence turn-on detection of alkaline phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Chen, J.H. Zhao, Y.Z. Lu, J. Sun, X.R. Yang, Fluorescence immunoassay based on the phosphate-triggered fluorescence turn-on detection of alkaline phosphatase, Anal Chem, 90 (2018) 3505-3511.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Logically regulating peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoclusters for sensing phosphate-containing metabolites and alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Chen, D. Zhao, Y.Y. Jiang, P.J. Ni, C.H. Zhang, B. Wang, F. Yang, Y.Z. Lu, J. Sun, Logically regulating peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoclusters for sensing phosphate-containing metabolites and alkaline phosphatase activity, Anal Chem, 91 (2019) 15017-15024.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y. Luo, J. Li, Y. Li, B. Yang, T. Zhou, J. Deng, Lanthanide-free infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles for real-time monitoring of alkaline phosphatase and its application for digital algal bloom detection, ACS Appl Nano Mater, 4 (2021) 11134-44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Horseradish peroxidase sol-gel immobilized for chemiluminescence measurements of alkaline-phosphatase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.N. Diaz, F.G. Sanchez, M.C. Ramos, M.C. Torijas, Horseradish peroxidase sol-gel immobilized for chemiluminescence measurements of alkaline-phosphatase activity, Sensor Actuat B-Chem, 82 (2002) 176-179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pyrophosphate as substrate for alkaline phosphatase activity: A convenient flow-injection chemiluminescence assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.F. Zhang, C.Y. Zhang, M.D. Yang, D.H. Yu, C. Yu, Pyrophosphate as substrate for alkaline phosphatase activity: A convenient flow-injection chemiluminescence assay, Luminescence, 32 (2017) 1150-1156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer immunoassay for alkaline phosphatase using p-nitrophenyl phosphate as substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Qi, Y.L. Fu, M.Y. Zhao, H.K. He, X. Tian, L.Z. Hu, Y. Zhang, Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer immunoassay for alkaline phosphatase using p-nitrophenyl phosphate as substrate, Anal Chim Acta, 1097 (2020) 71-77.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly sensitive homogeneous electrochemical assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on single molecular beacon-initiated T7 exonuclease-mediated signal amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.F. Zhang, T. Hou, H.Y. Li, F. Li, A highly sensitive homogeneous electrochemical assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on single molecular beacon-initiated T7 exonuclease-mediated signal amplification, Analyst, 140 (2015) 4030-4036.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H. Wang, F. Wang, T. Wu, Y. Liu, Highly active electrochemiluminescence of ruthenium complex co-assembled chalcogenide nanoclusters and the application for label-free detection of alkaline phosphatase, Anal Chem, 93 (2021) 15794-801.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reliable digital single molecule electrochemistry for ultrasensitive alkaline phosphatase detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, C.H. Zhou, L.J. Pan, T. Zeng, L. Zhu, D.W. Pang, Z.L. Zhang, Reliable digital single molecule electrochemistry for ultrasensitive alkaline phosphatase detection, Anal Chem, 88 (2016) 9166-9172.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. Liu, H. Meng, L. Zhang, S. Li, J. Chen, Y. Zhang, et al., Dual-readout test strips platform for portable and highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase in human serum samples, Chinese Chemical Letters, 32 (2021) 3421-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent turn-on probe for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.I. Kim, H. Kim, Y. Choi, Y. Kim, A fluorescent turn-on probe for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity in living cells, Chem Commun, 47 (2011) 9825-9827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of stable CdTe/CdS QDs using dithiol as surface ligand for alkaline phosphatase detection based on inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.B. Mao, Q. Zhang, Y.L. Yang, X.H. Ji, Z.K. He, Facile synthesis of stable CdTe/CdS QDs using dithiol as surface ligand for alkaline phosphatase detection based on inner filter effect, Anal Chim Acta, 1047 (2019) 208-213.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time ratiometric fluorescent assay for alkaline phosphatase activity with stimulus responsive infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.J. Deng, P. Yu, Y.X. Wang, L.Q. Mao, Real-time ratiometric fluorescent assay for alkaline phosphatase activity with stimulus responsive infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles, Anal Chem, 87 (2015) 3080-3086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.L. Qu, H.M. Pei, R.M. Kong, S.Y. Zhu, L. Xia, Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets, Talanta, 165 (2017) 136-142.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual and fluorescent detection of tyrosinase activity by using a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yan, H.X. Li, W.S. Zheng, X.G. Se, Visual and fluorescent detection of tyrosinase activity by using a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe, Anal Chem, 87 (2015) 8904-8909.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Yang, P.F. Shen, K.H. Tan, Y.S. Xia, Quantum dots and polymer hybrid composites: new insights into fluorescence switch and turn-on anion sensing, Microchim Acta, 181 (2014) 607-613.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.Z. Chen, X.L. Ren, X.W. Meng, L.F. Tan, D. Chen, F.Q. Tang, Quantum dots-based fluorescent probes for turn-on and turn-off sensing of butyrylcholinesterase, Biosens Bioelectron, 44 (2013) 204-209.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.H. Zhao, S. Wang, S.S. Lu, G.Y. Liu, J. Sun, X.R. Yang, Fluorometric and colorimetric dual-readout immunoassay based on an alkaline phosphatase-triggered reaction, Anal Chem, 91 (2019) 7828-7834.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, J. Yu, P.N. Zhao, S.Y. Guo, S. Han, One-step synthesis of water-soluble CdS quantum dots for silver-ion detection, Acs Omega, 6 (2021) 7139-7146.
